headline: Haze Returns as 478 Hotspots Detected in Sumatra and Kalimantan
context: ASEAN Specialised Meteorological Centre recorded 478 hotspots across Sumatra and Kalimantan in September 2025, pushing Malaysia's API above 200 in 6 states for 18 days.

cards.big: The ASEAN Transboundary Haze Agreement was signed in 2002.
cards.sub: 23 years later, Malaysia's API exceeded 200 for 18 days. The agreement has no enforcement mechanism.

cards.big: Haze cost Malaysia an estimated RM4.2B in 2025.
cards.sub: Healthcare: RM1.1B. Productivity loss: RM1.8B. Tourism: RM0.9B. Aviation disruption: RM0.4B.

cards.big: 5 Malaysian-owned plantation companies operate in fire-affected areas.
cards.sub: No Malaysian company has been sanctioned under either Malaysian or Indonesian law for fire incidents.

cards.big: 92,000 Malaysians sought hospital treatment for haze-related conditions in 2025.
cards.sub: Children under 5 and adults over 65 accounted for 61% of admissions.

cards.big: An international agreement without penalties is an international suggestion. The haze returns because it can.
Malaysia cannot solve haze diplomatically alone. But it can hold Malaysian companies accountable for fires on their concessions abroad. It has chosen not to.
